title: 06_conv_linear_shape.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Ship the 10-Class Image Baseline

The vision team wants a from-scratch CNN baseline before anyone reaches for a pretrained backbone: two conv → ReLU → max-pool blocks for feature extraction, then a single fully connected head that emits 10 logits. The head was sized at design time — after the second pooling stage, each 32×32 RGB image has been distilled to 32 channels of 8×8 features, 2048 values in all — so the classifier reads `nn.Linear(2048, 10)`. A smoke-test cell pushes one batch of 64 images through the stack and asserts the logits land at (64, 10).

Instead, the very first forward pass dies with a `RuntimeError` about two matrices that cannot be multiplied, and the shape it reports receiving — 16384 rows of 8 values — appears nowhere in the design. Somewhere between the feature extractor and the classifier head, the tensor is not the shape anyone planned for. Decode the numbers in the message and repair the boundary.
initialCode:
import torch
from torch import nn

# smoke test: one batch of 64 RGB images, 32x32
images = torch.randn(64, 3, 32, 32)

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(3, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),      # -> (N, 16, 16, 16)
    nn.Conv2d(16, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),      # -> (N, 32, 8, 8)
    nn.Linear(2048, 10),  # head sized for 32*8*8 = 2048 features
)

logits = model(images)
print("logits shape:", tuple(logits.shape))
assert logits.shape == (64, 10), f"expected (64, 10), got {tuple(logits.shape)}"
traceback:

--> 253     input = module(input)

RuntimeError: mat1 and mat2 shapes cannot be multiplied (16384x8 and 2048x10)

options.label: Resize the head to nn.Linear(8, 10) so in_features matches the 8 in the error
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn

# smoke test: one batch of 64 RGB images, 32x32
images = torch.randn(64, 3, 32, 32)

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(3, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),      # -> (N, 16, 16, 16)
    nn.Conv2d(16, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),      # -> (N, 32, 8, 8)
    nn.Linear(8, 10),     # match the 8 reported in the error
)

logits = model(images)
print("logits shape:", tuple(logits.shape))
assert logits.shape == (64, 10), f"expected (64, 10), got {tuple(logits.shape)}"
options.resultLog:
logits shape: (64, 32, 8, 10)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 19
     17 logits = model(images)
     18 print("logits shape:", tuple(logits.shape))
---> 19 assert logits.shape == (64, 10), f"expected (64, 10), got {tuple(logits.shape)}"

AssertionError: expected (64, 10), got (64, 32, 8, 10)
options.rationale: It runs — and that is the trap. `nn.Linear` happily accepts any tensor whose *last* dimension equals its in_features, so the (64, 32, 8, 8) volume flows through and emerges as (64, 32, 8, 10): 256 independent ten-way classifiers per image, each looking at a single 8-value sliver of one channel row. The 8 in the error message was a symptom — the trailing dimension of an unflattened tensor — never the spec. Without the shape assert, this would have marched straight into a loss function and trained nonsense.

options.label: Flatten the image batch before it enters the network
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn

# smoke test: one batch of 64 RGB images, 32x32
images = torch.randn(64, 3, 32, 32)

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(3, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),      # -> (N, 16, 16, 16)
    nn.Conv2d(16, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),      # -> (N, 32, 8, 8)
    nn.Linear(2048, 10),  # head sized for 32*8*8 = 2048 features
)

logits = model(images.flatten(start_dim=1))
print("logits shape:", tuple(logits.shape))
assert logits.shape == (64, 10), f"expected (64, 10), got {tuple(logits.shape)}"

File ~/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py:1778, in Module._wrapped_call_impl(self, *args, **kwargs)
   1776     return self._compiled_call_impl(*args, **kwargs)  # type: ignore[misc]
   1777 else:
-> 1778     return self._call_impl(*args, **kwargs)

File ~/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/conv.py:565, in Conv2d.forward(self, input)
    564 def forward(self, input: Tensor) -> Tensor:
--> 565     return self._conv_forward(input, self.weight, self.bias)

RuntimeError: Expected 3D (unbatched) or 4D (batched) input to conv2d, but got input of size: [64, 3072]
options.rationale: Flattening before the network destroys exactly the structure the convolutions exist to exploit: `Conv2d` demands a (N, C, H, W) volume and refuses the (64, 3072) matrix outright. The rank change belongs *after* spatial feature extraction is finished, not before it begins — conv layers speak 4-D, classifier heads speak 2-D, and the conversion lives at the seam between them, never at the input.

options.label: Insert nn.Flatten() between the conv stack and the head, sized 32 * 8 * 8 = 2048
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn

# smoke test: one batch of 64 RGB images, 32x32
images = torch.randn(64, 3, 32, 32)

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(3, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),      # -> (N, 16, 16, 16)
    nn.Conv2d(16, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),      # -> (N, 32, 8, 8)
    nn.Flatten(),         # -> (N, 32 * 8 * 8) = (N, 2048)
    nn.Linear(32 * 8 * 8, 10),  # 2048 flattened features -> 10 logits
)

logits = model(images)
print("logits shape:", tuple(logits.shape))
assert logits.shape == (64, 10), f"expected (64, 10), got {tuple(logits.shape)}"
options.rationale: `nn.Flatten()` collapses everything after the batch dimension: (64, 32, 8, 8) becomes (64, 2048), exactly the (N, in_features) contract a fully connected head expects. Writing the head as `nn.Linear(32 * 8 * 8, 10)` documents where 2048 comes from — 32 channels times an 8×8 spatial map — so whoever next touches a kernel size, stride, or pooling stage can re-derive the number instead of trusting a magic constant.

hints: A convolution stack hands its result onward as a 4-D tensor — (batch, channels, height, width) — while a fully connected layer contracts exactly one flat feature dimension per sample. When the two meet with no explicit rank change between them, the matrix multiply receives dimensions that were never meant to line up; the folded numbers in the error tell you precisely what arrived.

Lines 13–14: the second `MaxPool2d` emits (N, 32, 8, 8), and `nn.Linear(2048, 10)` receives that volume as-is. A Linear only ever contracts the *last* dimension of its input — size 8 here — so the 2048 the head was built for never gets a chance to participate. Something that turns the volume into a vector is missing at that seam.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`nn.Linear` treats only the *last* dimension of its input as features. Handed the conv stack's (64, 32, 8, 8) output, it tries to contract that trailing 8 against its (2048 × 10) weight — and to set up the multiply, PyTorch folds every leading dimension into rows: 64 × 32 × 8 = 16384 of them. That is exactly what the error reports: `(16384x8 and 2048x10)`. The network really does produce 2048 values per image, but as a (32, 8, 8) *volume*, and nothing in the `Sequential` converts that volume into the (N, 2048) matrix the head was designed around.

### Why the fix is correct
`nn.Flatten()` collapses every dimension after the batch axis — (64, 32, 8, 8) → (64, 2048) — which is precisely the (N, in_features) contract a classifier head expects, while leaving the batch dimension untouched. Spelling the head as `nn.Linear(32 * 8 * 8, 10)` documents where 2048 comes from, so the next person who changes a kernel size, stride, or pooling stage can re-derive the number instead of inheriting a magic constant. The general principle: every conv→linear boundary needs an explicit rank change, and the in_features on the far side is channels × height × width at that exact point.
recruiterReview: Clean diagnosis. You read the folded shapes in the error — 16384×8 is 64×32×8 rows of a tensor nobody flattened — instead of pattern-matching on the nearest number, which is exactly how option A ships a model that trains on noise. The principle: conv stacks and linear heads have different shape contracts, and the boundary between them needs an explicit, documented rank change. Sizing the head as 32 * 8 * 8 keeps that arithmetic auditable in review. Forward-looking: put an `nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((1, 1))` (or `nn.LazyLinear`) in front of the head if input resolution might ever change — it makes the classifier immune to this entire class of drift. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## The Conv→Linear Boundary: Where Shape Bugs Live

Convolutional layers and fully connected layers are both "just matrix math," but they disagree about what a batch looks like, and the seam between them is the single most common crash site in beginner PyTorch. Understanding the two contracts — and how to read the error when they collide — turns a cryptic RuntimeError into a thirty-second fix.

### Two layer families, two shape contracts
`Conv2d`, `MaxPool2d`, and `BatchNorm2d` speak **(N, C, H, W)**: a batch of feature *volumes*, where channels, height, and width all carry meaning. `nn.Linear` speaks **(N, in_features)** — more precisely `(*, in_features)`: it contracts only the *last* dimension against its weight matrix and treats every leading dimension as batch. Nothing in PyTorch converts between the two automatically. A `Sequential` will happily hand a 4-D volume to a Linear, and the result is either a multiplication error or — worse — a silently wrong computation whenever the trailing dimension happens to equal `in_features`.

### How to read the mat1/mat2 error
`mat1 and mat2 shapes cannot be multiplied (16384x8 and 2048x10)` encodes the whole diagnosis. mat2 is always the Linear's weight as (in_features, out_features) — here, the head expecting 2048. mat1 is your input after PyTorch folds all leading dimensions into rows: 64 × 32 × 8 = 16384 rows of 8 values each. So the message literally says "a 4-D tensor with trailing dimension 8 arrived where 2048 features were expected." Quick heuristic: if mat1's row count is not your batch size, an unflattened tensor is almost always the culprit.

### Sizing the head: make the arithmetic visible
The flattened size is channels × height × width at the boundary, so track shapes through the stack: a 3×3 conv with `padding=1` preserves H and W; `MaxPool2d(2)` halves them. 32×32 → pool → 16×16 → pool → 8×8, with 32 channels: 32 · 8 · 8 = 2048. Write that as `nn.Linear(32 * 8 * 8, num_classes)` so the derivation lives in the code. When you would rather not hand-compute: run a dummy forward (`features(torch.zeros(1, 3, 32, 32)).shape`), print a `torchinfo` summary, or use `nn.LazyLinear`, which infers `in_features` on the first call. `nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((1, 1))` ahead of the head goes further still — it makes the classifier independent of input resolution entirely, which is why most torchvision backbones end with it.

### Where the flatten goes — and where it must not
The rank change belongs after the last spatial layer and before the first Linear: `nn.Flatten()` inside a `Sequential` (it defaults to `start_dim=1`, preserving the batch axis), or `x = x.view(x.size(0), -1)` in a hand-written `forward`. Prefer `view(x.size(0), -1)` over `view(-1, F)`: the first fails loudly if the feature count drifts, the second silently rebatches your data. And never flatten *before* the convolutions — they exist to exploit the spatial structure that flattening destroys.

### Common variations of this bug
- Flatten present, but the head sized for a different input resolution — trained on 32×32, crashes (or worse) on 64×64.
- Changing a kernel size, stride, or padding without re-deriving the head's `in_features`.
- `view(-1, features)` silently changing the batch size instead of erroring when upstream shapes drift.
- Transfer-learning surgery that swaps in a new classifier but drops the original model's pooling or flatten step.
- Feeding channels-last arrays from NumPy or PIL — (N, H, W, C) — so every downstream shape computation is wrong from the start.
